Name: optimized_sparse_kernels_for_llm_inference
Title: Optimized Sparse Kernels for Efficient Large Language Model Inference on GPUs
Short Hypothesis: By optimizing sparse matrix multiplication (SpMM) kernels at the kernel level, we can significantly reduce the memory and computational overhead of large language model (LLM) inference on GPUs, leading to faster and more efficient inference.
Related Work: Recent works such as SpInfer [1] and At-Scale Sparse Deep Neural Network Inference [2] have explored the use of sparse matrices to reduce the memory and computational requirements of LLMs. However, these approaches often suffer from inefficiencies in kernel-level optimizations and suboptimal use of GPU resources. Our proposal aims to address these limitations by focusing on kernel-level optimizations that leverage GPU-specific features such as Tensor Cores and shared memory.
Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) have revolutionized natural language processing but are constrained by their high memory and computational demands. Sparse matrix multiplication (SpMM) is a promising technique to reduce these requirements, but existing implementations often fail to fully leverage GPU capabilities. We propose a novel framework, SparseInfer, which optimizes SpMM kernels at the kernel level to enhance LLM inference efficiency on GPUs. SparseInfer introduces Tensor-Core-Aware Bitmap Encoding (TCA-BME) to minimize indexing overhead and an optimized SpMM kernel with Shared Memory Bitmap Decoding (SMBD) to improve computational efficiency. Our experiments show that SparseInfer achieves significant speedups over state-of-the-art methods, reducing inference latency and memory usage while maintaining high accuracy.
Experiments: 1. **Kernel Optimization**: Develop and optimize a new SpMM kernel that leverages Tensor Cores and shared memory. Measure the performance gains in terms of achieved bandwidth, arithmetic intensity, and warp stall reasons using NVProf and Nsight.

2. **Tensor-Core-Aware Bitmap Encoding (TCA-BME)**: Implement and evaluate the TCA-BME format to minimize indexing overhead. Compare the performance of TCA-BME with existing sparse formats such as CSR and COO.
3. **Shared Memory Bitmap Decoding (SMBD)**: Design and test the SMBD mechanism to enhance computational efficiency. Measure the impact of SMBD on kernel performance and memory usage.

4. **End-to-End Inference**: Integrate the optimized SpMM kernel into an LLM inference pipeline and evaluate the end-to-end performance on a commodity GPU. Compare the latency, throughput, and energy consumption with state-of-the-art methods such as SpInfer and Flash-LLM.

5. **Scalability and Robustness**: Test the scalability of SparseInfer across multiple GPUs and different sparsity levels. Evaluate the robustness of the system under varying input distributions and model sizes.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1. **Complexity of Kernel Optimization**: Optimizing SpMM kernels at the kernel level can be complex and time-consuming, requiring extensive profiling and tuning.
2. **Memory Overhead**: While TCA-BME aims to minimize indexing overhead, there is a risk that the bitmap encoding itself may introduce additional memory requirements.
3. **Generalization to Different Models**: The effectiveness of SparseInfer may vary across different LLM architectures and input distributions, requiring further experimentation and tuning.
4. **Hardware Dependence**: The performance gains observed may be highly dependent on the specific GPU architecture used, limiting the generalizability of the results.
